currentUse: Private residential condominiums (reported as 15 units, including three affordable) on St. James Avenue in South Norwood; no Lithuanian signage or markers remain on the building
summary: St. George (Šv. Jurgio) Lithuanian Catholic Church, 37 St. James Avenue, South Norwood — built in 1915 and dedicated in September 1916 — was closed in the Archdiocese of Boston's 2004 reconfiguration, listed among 13 parish closings in the archdiocese's South Region; its last formal Mass was held in 2004 and its final pastor was Father William Wolkovich, historian of Norwood's Lithuanians. The building still stands but has been converted to residential condominiums: per Boston Globe reporting, the property was purchased by Karsten Co. in 2005 for $1 million and 15 condominiums (three affordable) were completed between 2008 and 2010. Nothing Lithuanian remains visible at the site. The 1949 monument to the parish's Lithuanian-American WWII veterans, which stood in front of the church, was relocated to Highland Cemetery in Norwood after the closure and survives there. The adjacent Lithuanian club closed much earlier, in 1978. The Norwood Historical Society published a retrospective on the parish in February 2023, indicating continued local memory work but no active preservation or reunion campaign was found.

developments.headline: St. George listed for closure in Archdiocese of Boston reconfiguration
developments.detail: The Boston.com special report on the archdiocese's 2004 reconfiguration lists 'Norwood, St. George' among 13 parish closings in the South Region, part of the roughly 70 parishes suppressed archdiocese-wide. The Norwood Historical Society records that the church held its last formal Mass in 2004, ending almost ninety years of service to South Norwood's Lithuanian community; Father William Wolkovich was the last pastor.

sources.title: St James Avenue to St George Avenue

developments.headline: Property sold to developer Karsten Co. for $1 million
developments.detail: Per the Boston Globe's 2014 tenth-anniversary retrospective on the reconfiguration, the St. George property was purchased by Karsten Co. in 2005 for $1 million. (Full article text is paywalled; figures are as attributed to the Globe article in search excerpts — see gaps.)

sources.title: A decade after closing, new life as condos and apartments for historic Catholic parishes

developments.headline: Church converted to 15 condominiums, including three affordable units
developments.detail: The Globe retrospective reports the residences were completed from 2008 to 2010, transforming St. George into 15 condominiums — three of them affordable — amid the two- and three-family homes off Washington Street. The Norwood Historical Society confirms the church 'has been converted to condominiums,' and Global True Lithuania confirms the building is now residential with no Lithuanian elements remaining. Current condo listings appear at 27-35 St. James Ave, Norwood MA 02062.

sources.title: A decade after closing, new life as condos and apartments for historic Catholic parishes
St James Avenue to St George Avenue
Boston, Massachusetts: Lithuanians and Lithuanian heritage

developments.headline: 1949 Lithuanian WWII veterans monument relocated to Highland Cemetery
developments.detail: The monument honoring 152 Lithuanian-American WWII veterans of St. George parish (eight killed in action listed by name), erected in 1949 in front of the church, was moved to Highland Cemetery in Norwood after the closure and survives as the town's remaining public Lithuanian marker. Back inscription: 'In faithful tribute to the Lithuanian veterans of Norwood.'

sources.title: Boston, Massachusetts: Lithuanians and Lithuanian heritage
St James Avenue to St George Avenue

developments.headline: Norwood Historical Society publishes parish retrospective
developments.detail: The Norwood Historical Society published a detailed history of the parish ('St James Avenue to St George Avenue'), covering its 1912 founding through the St. George Society, the 1915 construction and September 1916 dedication, the 1926 rectory, 1950s school (closed by 1985), Father Wolkovich's tenure, and the condominium conversion — the most recent substantive community attention to the parish found.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: One refinement, no contradictions. The archive note says the church was 'converted to an ordinary residential house' — the verified record is more specific: it was converted to a multi-unit condominium development (reported as 15 units, three affordable, completed 2008-2010), not a single-family house. 'Lithuanian signs removed' is confirmed (Global True Lithuania: nothing Lithuanian remains at the building). 'Closed' and 'diocese_closed' are confirmed and can be dated: closed in the Archdiocese of Boston's 2004 reconfiguration (last formal Mass 2004), listed on the archdiocese's South Region closings list. The archive's missing founding date can be filled: parish organized 1912 (land purchased via the St. George Society; titles transferred to the Archdiocese 1913), church built 1915, dedicated September 1916.
gaps: The Boston Globe 2014 article carrying the sale details (Karsten Co., $1 million, 2005; 15 condos incl. 3 affordable; completed 2008-2010) could not be opened in full — the story URL is paywalled/404 and the details come from consistent search excerpts of that article across two independent queries; treat the exact price and buyer name as Globe-attributed but not independently re-verified against the full text or registry of deeds. Address ambiguity: the Norwood Historical Society gives the church as 37 St. James Avenue (rectory 35), while current condo listings appear as 27-35 St. James Ave — the exact condominium association name and address numbering were not confirmed. The precise date of the last Mass in 2004 and the exact date the WWII monument was moved to Highland Cemetery were not found. No exact closure decree date, no press on reunions, alumni gatherings, or preservation campaigns was found (searched Draugas, Boston Globe, local press). Fate of the rectory, convent (1950), and former school building was not established. Identity check: this is unambiguously the Lithuanian St. George parish — Norwood's Antiochian Orthodox St. George parish (Atwood Ave) is a different congregation and was excluded.
